text:
King's College Hospital 

Department of Neurosciences 

Clinic Date: 09 September 2017 

Dr Amelia Carter
Consultant Neurologist  
King's College Hospital  
Department of Neurosciences, King's College Hospital, London SE5 9RS  

Dear Dr Amelia Carter
Consultant Neurologist,  

Daniel Harper, DOB: 14-03-1982, Hospital No: K732914 NHS No. 9075314682  
Flat 2, 18 Brookfield Road, London, SE15 4BQ

Thank you for your referral and for forwarding background details regarding hearing difficulties and communication preferences. I reviewed the patient in clinic today. Given his reduced hearing, we arranged an extended appointment with written prompts and followed up via scheduled speakerphone with his sister present, as he prefers a family member to assist. He was comfortable with this approach and felt it improved understanding of the plan.

He described a long-standing seizure history with variable semiologies reported in past notes; however, he has found it challenging to characterise events consistently. Importantly, he volunteered that his last clear generalised convulsive event occurred in 2016 and that he has not had any collapse episodes since. Specifically, he stated: "Last tonic-clonic seizure was in May-2016, with 2 to 3 morning jerks since then." The morning jerks are brief, predominantly on waking, without loss of awareness, and do not cluster. He denies nocturnal events witnessed by family, tongue biting, or post-event confusion in the last year. There is no reported aura. Triggers are uncertain; sleep disruption occasionally precedes the morning jerks. He does not drive.

He is adherent with current medication and uses a phone reminder system set up by his nephew. No clear adverse effects were reported beyond mild daytime fatigue. Alcohol intake is minimal and he has begun a regular sleep schedule on family advice. There is no recent head injury, infection, or new systemic illness. He requested that future communications, including test appointments, be arranged as pre-booked speakerphone calls with his sister present.

On examination today he was alert and cooperative with good effort. Speech and language were normal in quiet surroundings with amplification; cranial nerves intact; motor and sensory examinations were normal; coordination and gait were steady. Cardiovascular and general examinations were unremarkable.

Given the long interval since the last convulsive event and the current pattern of brief morning jerks without impairment, I have advised continued observation and medication adherence. I have requested baseline bloods for safety monitoring and will obtain prior EEG and MRI reports from your records to consolidate the historical data. We discussed rescue arrangements should his events evolve; he and his sister understand when to seek urgent help. We also agreed a seizure and sleep diary to clarify frequency and potential triggers of the morning jerks.

Communication plan: we will schedule follow-up via speakerphone in 4 months, with his sister present, and will send written summaries in accessible format. He prefers appointment reminders by text followed by a pre-arranged call.

Thank you again for your referral and for supporting the adapted communication arrangements. Please let me know if there are further concerns or additional history from the family that would help guide ongoing management.

Yours sincerely

entities.text: Last tonic-clonic seizure was in May-2016, with 2 to 3 morning jerks since then.
Last tonic-clonic seizure was in May-2016, with 2 to 3 morning jerks since then

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis:
- Identify explicit event counts: The letter states, "Last tonic-clonic seizure was in May-2016, with 2 to 3 morning jerks since then." This implies 1 generalized convulsive seizure in May 2016 plus an additional 2 to 3 morning jerks after that.
- Determine the time window: From May 2016 to the clinic date 09 September 2017 is approximately 16 months (12 months to May 2017 + 4 months to September 2017).
- Treat morning jerks as seizure events (managed within a seizure plan and diary). There are no other events reported (denies nocturnal events, collapse episodes).
- Total seizures over the interval: 1 (tonic-clonic) + 2 to 3 (morning jerks) = 3 to 4 events over ~16 months.
- Convert to allowed format: "3 to 4 per 16 month."
seizure_frequency_number: Last tonic-clonic seizure was in May-2016, with 2 to 3 morning jerks since then.
reference: Last tonic-clonic seizure was in May-2016, with 2 to 3 morning jerks since then
